speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're getting comfortable inside GitHub repositories: how they're laid out, how to orient yourself quickly with a screen reader or keyboard, and how to move from, "I found a repo," to, "I actually know where things are."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is one of those chapters where the goal is not speed, right? It's confidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is a foundation lesson. There are no challenge tasks here, no automation checks. The point is to build a reliable mental map before later chapters ask you to work with issues, pull requests, and traceable contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone's feeling pressure to perform already, this is a good place to exhale.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Think of this as orientation practice. Learn the page structure, practice basic movement keys like heading navigation and landmark navigation, confirm you feel ready with a peer or facilitator if you have one, and then move on. It's a safety-first learning pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we dive in, what companion resources matter here?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few. There's an episode version of this lesson for preview or review. There's a glossary appendix and a screen reader cheat sheet appendix. And there are two especially useful outside references: GitHub Docs on repositories, and GitHub's own accessibility guide for using repositories with a screen reader.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that official accessibility guide is NVDA-focused, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. This lesson covers the same general territory but adds workshop guidance, VoiceOver perspectives, low-vision workflows, and some command-line connection points.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I also noticed the chapter talks about learning cards.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Throughout the written material, there are expandable learning cards. Not every card appears at every step. They're there so people can open the guidance that fits how they work. The four types are: visual and mouse guidance, low vision guidance for zoom or high contrast users, screen reader guidance for NVDA or JAWS on Windows, and screen reader guidance for VoiceOver on macOS.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the lesson is shared, but the access path can differ.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the basic question: what is a repository page?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because people hear "repo" all the time, but the page itself has a structure.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. When you visit a URL like github dot com slash owner slash repo-name, you usually land on the repository home page, which is also the Code tab. That page has several dependable parts even if the visual arrangement shifts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "even if" seems important for accessibility.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very important. GitHub can change visual placement depending on screen width, account features, repository settings, and ongoing product changes. So instead of memorizing a fixed visual layout, it's better to rely on stable structural cues: the global navigation, the repository's main heading, the repository navigation landmark, the Code tab, the branch selector, the file list, an optional About sidebar, and often a rendered README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the visual order might look familiar on a wide desktop screen, but learners shouldn't treat that as a promise.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. On a big screen you might often perceive: global nav, repo header, tab row, file and code panel, optional sidebar, then README content. But for accessible navigation, use headings, landmarks, tab names, and shortcuts to re-orient instead of trusting a visual map alone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay. I open a repository for the first time. What should I expect to hear or find first?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three big things. First, the page title is often announced in a format like owner slash repo-name, then a short description, then GitHub. Second, if you press the number 1 key to jump to the first level-one heading, you should land on the repo name. Third, the tab bar lives in a landmark labeled Repository navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's an orientation routine the chapter wants people to repeat on every new repo.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and it's worth making habitual. Step one: press 1 and hear the repository name. Step two: press D to move through landmarks and learn the page structure. Step three: open your headings list--NVDA plus F7, or VoiceOver plus U and then look at headings--to understand what sections exist on this page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds simple, but it's really a way to reduce that "where am I?" feeling.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And as you move through landmarks with D, some key names often appear. Repository Navigation is the tab bar. Main contains the file tree, branch selector, repo details, and contributors. Repository Files Navigation often contains the rendered README content. Within each landmark, headings--often level 2--help break things into chunks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about the tabs. Those are easy to overlook if you're overwhelmed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Repository tabs are a major control surface. Common tabs include Code, Issues, Pull Requests, Discussions, Actions, Projects, Wiki, Security, Insights, and Settings. But not every repository shows all of them. Discussions, Wiki, and Projects have to be enabled, and Settings is usually only visible to maintainers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do people reach them accessibly?

speaker: ALEX
text: For screen reader users on Windows, press D to jump to the Repository navigation landmark, then use K for links or Tab to move between tab links. For VoiceOver, use the rotor to jump to landmarks, find Repository navigation, then move through items within it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low-vision users?

speaker: ALEX
text: At higher zoom levels, the tabs may wrap onto two lines, but they remain standard links. The active tab is typically shown with an underline. In Windows High Contrast mode, that underline uses the system accent color. If it's hard to click precisely at high zoom, Tab through the links instead.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also mentions shortcuts to jump straight to some tabs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. GitHub supports some two-key shortcuts. G then I jumps to Issues. G then P jumps to Pull Requests. Those are handy when you already know where you want to go.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what do tab labels sound like with a screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually the tab name plus a count. For example, "Issues, 14 open," or "Pull requests, 3 open." If the tab is the one you're currently on, the screen reader may announce it as selected or current.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now we're on the Code tab. What's the centerpiece there?

speaker: ALEX
text: The files table. This is the heart of the repository home page. It lists the folders and files in the current location.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you reach it?

speaker: ALEX
text: For screen reader users, one fast method is pressing T to jump to the next table on the page. The first table is often the files table, and you may hear something like, "table with N rows and 3 columns."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And visually?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's usually just below the branch selector in the main content area. It generally has three columns: Name, Message, and Age or Date. Name is the file or folder name. Message is the most recent commit message affecting that item. Age is how long ago that change happened.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's more informative than just a file listing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It tells you not only what exists, but also recent activity. That helps you identify active areas of a project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about people using magnification?

speaker: ALEX
text: At 200 percent zoom or more, the message and date columns may truncate. Tooltips may reveal full text on hover. The icons for folders and files remain visible, and in high contrast they should use system colors with visible outlines. The chapter also suggests using browser Find, Control plus F, to locate a visible file name on the page, and using Go to file for faster searching.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once I'm in the table, how do I actually read it well with a screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think row by row. Move to the Name column first, hear the file or folder name, move right to hear the latest commit message, and right again to hear the age. So a row might effectively read as, "docs slash, Add accessibility guide, 3 days ago."

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are table navigation keys depending on the screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For NVDA and JAWS, people often use Control Alt with the arrow keys to move among cells. For VoiceOver, VO Shift with arrow keys is common in tables. To open a file or folder, activate the link in the Name column--Enter on Windows, or VO Space on Mac.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you tell folders from files?

speaker: ALEX
text: Folders end with a slash in the Name column. Opening a folder reloads the page to show that folder's contents. To move back up, use the browser Back button or the breadcrumb links if present.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So one little slash matters a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: It really does.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the learning-card spirit here is basically: jump to the table, confirm columns, read rows in order, and activate only from the name cell.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That pattern keeps you oriented.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next up: branches. People hear "branch" early in Git, but on the repository page there's a specific control for it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, the branch selector. It lets you switch which branch--or sometimes tag--you're viewing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you open it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually there's a button near the top of the file listing area labeled something like the current branch name, often main. Activating it opens a dropdown or dialog for switching branches and tags.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's inside that dropdown?

speaker: ALEX
text: Typically a search field and lists for branches and tags. You can type to filter results. This is useful in repositories with many branches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And tags matter because they often represent releases.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Switching to a tag lets you view the repository as it existed at a specific labeled point, often a release version. That's very useful when you want to inspect what was in a release without following ongoing work on the main branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's connect that to cloning, because people often confuse "I can view it in the browser" with "I have it on my computer."

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Cloning means making a local copy of the repository on your machine so you can inspect or work with it using Git and your editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter has a nice practical angle here: you don't always need the full URL.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. With GitHub CLI, you can often clone using the repository owner and name directly--something like gh repo clone owner slash repo-name--without manually copying a URL. And a common pattern is to clone and then immediately change into the new folder.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if you're using standard Git instead of GitHub CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you'd use git clone with an HTTPS or SSH URL, depending on your setup. The concept is the same: Git downloads the repository history and working files into a local directory.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the main teaching points from the cloning learning cards and tool cards?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, know what tool you're using: browser only, GitHub CLI, or standard Git. Second, understand that cloning is local; it does not create a separate GitHub copy. Third, after cloning, you typically cd into the folder so your terminal context matches the repository. And fourth, if you want to contribute but don't have write access, cloning alone may not be enough--you may also need a fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Perfect transition: fork versus clone versus branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: This distinction is foundational. A clone is a local copy on your own computer. A fork is a copy of someone else's repository under your own GitHub account. A branch is a parallel line of work inside one repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I say, "I made a branch," I did not make a new repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. And if you say, "I cloned the repo," you probably still mean the same repository, just locally on disk. If you say, "I forked it," you now have your own GitHub-hosted copy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When does each one matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Clone when you want local access. Fork when you need your own remote copy, especially for contributing to a project where you don't have direct write permissions. Branch when you want to isolate a feature, fix, or experiment within a repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That clears up a lot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The repository page also has those social or workflow buttons: watch, star, fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and they do different jobs. Watching subscribes you to notifications. Starring bookmarks the repository for later and signals interest. Forking creates your own copy under your account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So starring is not subscribing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If you star a project, you're saying, "I want to remember this." If you watch it, you're saying, "Tell me what's happening here." If you fork it, you're saying, "I want my own copy to work from."

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a really useful three-part distinction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now suppose I activate a file from the files table. What does a single file page look like?

speaker: ALEX
text: A file page has its own landmarks and structure. You'll encounter the file path, file content, and action buttons for operations like raw view, blame, history, edit, download, or copy link, depending on permissions and file type.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's break that into markdown files versus code files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. If you open a Markdown file like README dot md, GitHub often shows a rendered reading view. That means headings, lists, links, and code blocks are presented in a more readable document format.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which can be much easier to navigate with heading commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A rendered README often behaves like a structured document inside the repository page. You can use heading navigation, links lists, and other document-reading strategies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about code files?

speaker: ALEX
text: Code files are typically shown line by line, often with line numbers. The reading experience is denser. Depending on your screen reader and mode, you may move through lines, review syntax-heavy text, and use the file controls to switch to raw view or blame if that helps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And those action buttons--how do people reach them?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually by navigating near the file header area, around the file title and controls. With a screen reader, landmark and heading navigation can get you close, then Tab or link/button navigation can reach the specific actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kinds of actions should learners expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common ones include viewing raw file contents, opening blame, viewing history, editing the file if you have permission or are working in your fork, and sometimes downloading. The exact set can vary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Editing from the browser sounds convenient, but also potentially risky if you're still learning.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's why the chapter's tone is careful. Browser editing exists, and it can be useful for small changes, especially in your own fork or for minor documentation fixes. But learners should stay oriented: know which branch they're on, know whether they're editing upstream or their own fork, and understand that saving in the browser creates a commit path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card message is: read the file structure first, then use action buttons intentionally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also the blame view, which has a dramatic name.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does, but it's very practical. Blame shows each line of a file annotated with the commit and author who last changed that line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I want to know who touched a section recently, blame is the tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It connects lines of code or text back to specific commits. That can help you understand why a change happened, who made it, and whether a pattern in the file is recent or old.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you navigate it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carefully and in chunks. The page often pairs commit metadata with file lines. Screen reader users may need to move between links, line annotations, and code content, so headings, landmarks, and structured navigation become extra important. It's less about speed and more about tracing context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then we have commit history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A repository or file can expose commit history, and it's one of the best ways to understand project activity over time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the commits list page usually contain?

speaker: ALEX
text: A list of commits, often with commit messages, authors, dates, and links to individual commit pages. Reading the list gives you a sense of project rhythm: frequent changes, documentation work, bug fixes, release preparation, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And an individual commit page?

speaker: ALEX
text: That page usually includes the commit message, metadata like author and time, the commit identifier, parent relationships, and a diff showing what changed. The diff is where you inspect additions and deletions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the commits list tells you what happened broadly, and the commit page tells you exactly what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Searching for a file is another huge practical skill, especially in large repositories.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. The fastest browser-based method is often Go to file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does that work?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub supports a keyboard shortcut--T on many repository pages--to open a file finder. You start typing part of a file name or path, the list filters as you type, and then you select the result you want.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That beats arrowing through dozens or hundreds of rows in a files table.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It's especially helpful at high magnification, for screen reader users who want to reduce navigation load, and for anyone working in a big codebase. The learning-card takeaway is simple: if you know even part of the file name, use Go to file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also calls out GitHub shortcuts in a kind of spotlight section.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Shortcuts can save a lot of effort once you're already oriented. We already mentioned G then I for Issues, G then P for Pull Requests, and T for Go to file. The broader lesson is not to memorize everything at once, but to adopt a few that reduce friction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also mention of navigating the sidebar.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Some repository pages include a sidebar with About information, links, releases, packages, contributor summaries, or related metadata. Depending on viewport and page design, it may appear before or after the main content in reading order. Again, don't rely on sighted layout assumptions--use headings and landmarks to find it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's focus on that About section for a moment. Why does it matter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because it often answers the first practical questions: What does this project do? Where is its homepage? What topics or tags describe it? Does it have releases or package information? The About section is quick orientation metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm evaluating whether a repo is even relevant to me, About is part of the answer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, along with the description and README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I love the practical scenarios section because it translates features into real intentions. Can we walk through those?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario A: "I want to find out what this project does."

speaker: ALEX
text: Start with the repository name and short description. Then find the About section if present. Then read the README, especially its opening paragraphs and headings. If the README is rendered, heading navigation is your friend. This sequence usually gives you purpose, scope, installation hints, and whether the project is active.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario B: "I want to find a good file to edit."

speaker: ALEX
text: Begin on the files table. Look for documentation files like README, CONTRIBUTING, docs folders, or clearly named small files. Read the Message and Age columns to see which areas are active. Use Go to file if you know the likely target. And if the project has contribution guidance, read that before editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario C: "I want to know who has been working on this file recently."

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the file, then use History or Blame. History shows commits affecting the file over time. Blame shows who last changed each line. Together, they help you identify recent contributors and active sections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario D: "I want to understand what changed in the last release."

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the branch or tag selector to switch to a release tag if needed, look for Releases information, and read recent commit history or release notes. If the repository uses tags consistently, comparing current code to a tagged version can clarify what changed around that release.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Scenario E: "I want to contribute--where do I start?"

speaker: ALEX
text: First, understand the project: description, About, README. Next, check whether there's a CONTRIBUTING file, issue templates, or discussions about newcomer tasks. Then decide your workflow: if you lack write access, you may need to fork; in any case, you'll likely clone locally and work on a branch. And watch for repository norms in issues and pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone gets stuck while navigating?

speaker: ALEX
text: Return to basics. Press 1 to confirm the repository heading. Use D to cycle through landmarks. Open the headings list. If you're lost in a long page, go back to known structural anchors instead of pushing forward blindly. You can also reload the page, return to the Code tab, or use a direct shortcut like G then I if your goal has changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So getting unstuck is really a re-orientation skill.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Not a failure--just a normal part of accessible navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter ends with a try-it activity: the five-tab tour. What's the spirit of that exercise?

speaker: ALEX
text: To make navigation concrete. Visit a repository and intentionally tour several tabs--typically Code, Issues, Pull Requests, maybe Actions or Wiki if available, and then back again. The purpose is to feel how the tab bar behaves, how the page structure shifts, and how you can keep re-orienting with headings, landmarks, and shortcuts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not "memorize every control," but "practice moving with confidence."

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Learn the terrain by traversing it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can you summarize the most important mental model from this lesson?

speaker: ALEX
text: A repository page is not just a wall of links. It's a structured workspace with repeatable landmarks: heading, repository navigation, code and files area, optional sidebar, and file or README content. If you learn to orient with headings, landmarks, tabs, tables, and a few shortcuts, GitHub becomes much more predictable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the big workflow vocabulary?

speaker: ALEX
text: Clone is local. Fork is your own remote copy. Branch is an isolated line of work within a repository. Watch subscribes. Star bookmarks. Blame traces lines to commits. History traces files and repositories over time. Go to file helps you jump directly where you need to go.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a strong toolkit.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And it's enough to make the next chapters much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Thanks everyone. Take a little time to practice the orientation sequence on a real repository: first heading, landmarks, headings list, tabs, files table, and one file page. That repetition pays off.

speaker: ALEX
text: And if you need authoritative backup, GitHub Docs and GitHub's accessibility guide are excellent companions. We'll continue building from here.
